text: An incision is made in the right flank for future ileostomy placement. An 11mm trocar is inserted, followed by 6mm flexible trocars at the six o'clock and twelve o'clock positions. Bicurved and monocurved grasping forceps are introduced through these trocars. The small bowel is positioned above the right liver. The origin of the inferior mesenteric vein is exposed, and a monocurve coagulating hook frees the vein, which is then clipped using a straight 5mm clipper plier. Monocurve scissors are used for further dissection. The left mesocolon is dissected while respecting Toldt's fascia, with the surgeon maintaining ergonomic positions throughout.

The surgeon frees and clips the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin using monocurve scissors. The left mesocolon is dissected from the gyrotous fascia, and the sigmoid and left colon are detached from the lateral parietal wall. The superior limits of rectal mobilization are displayed on both sides. The left mesocolon is sectioned intercorporeally with the monocurve coagulating hook and monocurve grasping forceps.

The surgeon continues the procedure using transanal laparoscopy, introducing a reusable D-port through the anus. A silicon cap with three orifices is applied, and various instruments are inserted through specific lumens: a graduated straight grasping forceps at three o'clock, a monocurve grasping forceps at nine o'clock, and a monocurve needle holder with a suture at three o'clock. A purse string suture is applied two centimeters below the lesion, followed by an extracorporeal knot pushed inside the D-port. The monocurve scissors cut the suture, and the monocurve coagulating hook is introduced at three o'clock to start the total mesorectal excision (TME) with circumferential rectal mucosa transection.

The surgeon performs dissection by transfixing the rectal wall, starting posteriorly and then moving laterally on both sides while respecting the levator ani muscle plane. The lateral dissections are joined medially, continuing posteriorly and laterally in the correct cleavage plane. Finally, the dissection proceeds anteriorly, separating the rectum from the prostate in male patients or the vagina in female patients.

During the lateral dissection, the surgeon exposes and sections the inferior rectal artery between two five mm clips. The peritoneal sheet at the level of the promontorium is sectioned, connecting to the previous abdominal laparoscopic dissection. The peritoneal sheets on both the left and right lateral sides of the pelvis are also sectioned.

The surgeon completes the sectioning of the medial peritoneal sheet, concluding this step. A final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is displayed, showing the excised specimen being pushed into the pelvis and grasped by a transanal grasper.

The D-port is removed, and the specimen is extracted. The left colon is sectioned at the previously marked segment. A circular anvil of the stapler is inserted into the anus, and a purse string suture is performed on the rectal stump, taking the rectal wall in full thickness. The suture is extracted, and an external knot is pushed inside the D-port against the anvil. After cutting the suture, the D-port is removed, and the circular stapler is introduced into the anus to perform the anastomosis. An intraluminal check is conducted.

The surgeon uses a monocurved needle holder to close the pelvic peritoneal sheet, starting on the right side and progressing to the left side, securing each with an intracorporeal knot. The ileocecal valve is identified, and a 20-centimeter ileal loop is selected and positioned naturally. The procedure concludes with the placement of a temporary ileostomy at the site of a single incision.
